ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers toasted bread; no composite ham-and-cheese toast code exists. Closest defensible parent used for the bread base component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for ham and cheese toast composite dish.

serving.description: 1 slice of toast (approximately 2 slices bread with ham and cheese)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ham and cheese toast is approximately 120 g, consisting of two slices of toasted white bread layered with sliced processed ham and melted cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Ham and Cheese Toast — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05020054 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for ham and cheese sandwich (USDA FDC ID 172004) as a secondary reference; see notes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for ham and cheese toast as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is assigned based on the GI of white bread (GI ~70–72, University of Sydney GI Database), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. Ham and cheese toppings contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the bread base. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 33.3 × 1.2) / 100 = 27.97, rounded to 27.7 per 120 g serving (39.96 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 666 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g hard threshold for hypertension suitability
hypertension.guidance: Ham and cheese toast is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension; sodium at 666 mg/100 g (approximately 800 mg per 120 g serving) is driven by processed ham and cheese and substantially exceeds the recommended limit — patients should choose reduced-sodium alternatives or replace processed ham with fresh low-sodium protein.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base (GI ~70) with 33.3 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.5 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ham and cheese toast made on white bread produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike due to its high GI and low fibre content; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute wholemeal or rye bread, limit portion to one slice, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable to blunt glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (666 mg/100 g), elevated total fat (17.59 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~6.8 g/100 g from processed cheese and ham
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ham and cheese toast presents a high cardiovascular risk profile due to its combination of high sodium, saturated fat from processed cheese, and refined carbohydrate from white bread; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and consider wholegrain bread, reduced-fat cheese, and fresh lean protein alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham and cheese toast is a popular Western-influenced breakfast and snack item widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of two slices of white bread that are toasted until golden and crisp, then layered with sliced processed ham and melted processed cheese, often prepared with a spread of margarine or butter on the bread surface before toasting. The result is a warm, savoury dish with a crunchy exterior, soft interior, and a rich, salty flavour profile from the combination of ham and cheese. Per 100 g, it delivers 334 kcal, 10.6 g protein, 17.59 g fat, and 33.3 g carbohydrate with only 1.5 g dietary fibre, making it a calorie-dense, high-sodium (666 mg/100 g) food. The high sodium content is driven by the processed ham and processed cheese, both of which are inherently high in salt. The white bread base contributes a high glycaemic index (~70), leading to rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (sodium exceeds 600 mg/100 g threshold), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, low fibre, high refined carbohydrate), and cardiovascular disease (high sodium and saturated fat). Calcium is notably present at 131 mg/100 g from the cheese component. This dish is not halal-certified in its standard form due to the pork-derived ham. It is commonly served at breakfast or as a light meal in cafes, kopitiams, and fast-food outlets across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is ham and cheese toast healthy?
How many calories in ham and cheese toast?
Can diabetics eat ham and cheese toast?
Is ham and cheese toast high in sodium?
What is the GI of ham and cheese toast?
Is ham and cheese toast suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020054, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FDC SR Legacy as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA FDC for this composite dish): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and fatty acid sub-fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this composite dish and are not reliably available from a single approved source for this exact preparation. Saturated fat (6.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for a comparable ham and cheese sandwich composite (FDC ID 172004); this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB sub-fraction report if available. Trans fat is set to -1 as no approved source reports a confirmed value for this specific HPB entry; the input record's narrative reference to estimated trans fat from margarine (~0.2 g/100 g) is not confirmed by an approved source and has not been entered as a numeric value. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite GI measurement exists for this dish. Halal status is not confirmed as standard ham is pork-derived; consumers should verify halal certification at point of purchase.
